one_line_takeaway: TARO aggregates diffusion denoiser outputs across multiple noise scales into an affine coarse-to-fine purification target, improving adaptive-attack robustness over single-scale test-time optimization purifiers (especially under l_infinity and DiffBreak's low-frequency attack) while also exposing, via a graph-consistent adaptive-evaluation protocol, that a prior published purifier (CLIPure) had inflated robustness numbers caused by an incompletely differentiated (gradient-masked) attack.
key_finding: On CIFAR-10 with WRN-28-10 under PGD-EOT-20 l_infinity (eps=8/255), TARO-2 reaches 88.27+-1.6% robust accuracy (93.41+-1.1% clean) and TARO-3 reaches 88.57+-0.9% robust (93.45+-0.9% clean), versus AAOpt's 85.12+-0.7% robust (91.53+-0.9% clean) and the strongest prior adversarial-training baseline Gowal et al. 2021 at 66.01% robust; the undefended classifier is 0.0% robust. Under l2 (eps=0.5) PGD-EOT, raw zero-shot TARO is comparatively weak (TARO-2: 69.21+-0.7% robust) but TARO-2+Consistency reaches 88.99+-1.2%, the best l2 result in that table, and TARO-2-AA reaches 88.19+-1.2%, both beating AAOpt's 86.40+-0.4%. Under BPDA-EOT (CIFAR-10, l_infinity), TARO-3-AA achieves the strongest robust accuracy at 93.04+-0.8% (clean 93.59+-0.7%) versus AAOpt 91.36+-0.8%; on CIFAR-100 BPDA-EOT, TARO-3-AA reaches 72.41+-1.7% robust vs AAOpt 66.20+-1.8%. Under DiffBreak's low-frequency attack, standard diffusion purifiers (1-step denoise, DiffPure-EDM) collapse to 0.0% robust accuracy, while AAOpt gets 85.41+-4.4% and TARO-2-AA improves to 90.10+-1.5% (and 94.7+-1.4% vs AAOpt's 93.2+-2.9% under transfer-DiffBreak). Under the stricter full-reverse AutoAttack-EOT, TARO-2-AA reaches 87.50% robust (91.01% clean) vs AAOpt's 85.54%. The guidance-strength ablation (Table 1) shows l_infinity robustness peaking near gamma=1.5 (87.82% with consistency) then degrading at gamma=3.0 (down to ~55-58%), while l2 robustness keeps improving with larger gamma and benefits much more from the consistency regularizer (e.g., at gamma=1.5, consistency raises l2 robust accuracy from 69.33% to 89.05%). Separately, the paper's own re-evaluation of CLIPure under graph-consistent (create_graph=True) attacks shows its reported AutoAttack l_infinity robust accuracy of 91.1% (CLIPure-Cos) and 88.0% (CLIPure-Diff) both collapse to 0.0% once the attacker is allowed to differentiate through CLIPure's inner optimization loop -- demonstrating that a previously published purification defense's robustness claim was an artifact of an incompletely differentiated (gradient-masked) adaptive attack, not real robustness.
core_contribution: The paper introduces TARO (Temporal Adversarial Rectification Optimization), a test-time (inference-time) adversarial purification method for image classifiers that treats different diffusion-model noise levels (timesteps) as separate 'temporal experts.' Instead of using a single diffusion noise scale during purification (as prior score-based test-time optimization defenses do), TARO aggregates denoised outputs from multiple noise scales (K=2 or K=3 experts) into an affine coarse-to-fine target, derived from a temporal product-of-experts (PoE) view of diffusion scores. High-noise experts give globally smoothed structure that is less sensitive to fragile adversarial perturbations; low-noise experts restore fine, class-relevant detail. A guidance-strength parameter gamma controls how much the fine expert corrects the coarse expert's target, and the paper allows gamma to push the aggregation outside the convex hull (an 'affine extrapolation' beyond standard PoE) to get stronger corrections. The purified image is updated via a RED-style stop-gradient loss toward this target, optionally combined with an expert-consistency regularizer (penalizing disagreement between the multi-scale denoised estimates) and/or with AAOpt's learned adversarial-residual likelihood (yielding hybrid variants TARO-K-AA). The paper also proposes and applies a stricter 'graph-consistent' adaptive evaluation protocol for optimization-based purifiers, showing that if the attacker's gradient through the purifier's inner optimization loop is computed with create_graph=False (a common PyTorch default), the attack silently drops second-order terms and produces inflated robustness estimates.
threat_model: White-box, adaptive threat model against the full purifier+classifier composition (not against the classifier alone). Perturbations are norm-bounded: l_infinity (epsilon=8/255) and l2 (epsilon=0.5) on CIFAR-10/CIFAR-100/Imagenette/CIFAR-10-C. Attacks evaluated: PGD-EOT (20 iterations, 20 EOT samples), BPDA-EOT (50 iterations, 15 EOT samples), AutoAttack-EOT with a full-reverse (fully unrolled) optimization trajectory (128 samples), and DiffBreak (Kassis et al. 2025), a low-frequency, LPIPS-guided adaptive attack designed specifically to bypass diffusion purification, plus a transfer-DiffBreak variant. The paper explicitly targets gradient masking / obfuscated-gradient failure modes: it requires attackers to differentiate through the purifier's inner optimization loop (preserving second-order/Hessian-dependent terms via create_graph=True) rather than treating the purifier as a black box or detaching its inner gradients, since doing the latter falsely inflates measured robustness.
limitations: Authors' own stated limitations: TARO increases inference latency due to iterative multi-scale optimization (multiple denoiser evaluations per update step), and the correction strength gamma and consistency-regularization weight must be tuned per threat model (the ablations show gamma=1.5 is good for l_infinity but poor generalization at gamma=3.0, while l2 wants larger gamma plus consistency regularization) -- i.e. no single hyperparameter setting is robust across attack norms without tuning. Raw zero-shot TARO (without the AA likelihood or consistency term) is explicitly weaker under l2 attacks than under l_infinity attacks. The paper states these tradeoffs are discussed further in Appendix A.12 (not extracted in this review's read window).

quantitative_results: PGD-EOT-20 l_inf (eps=8/255) CIFAR-10 WRN-28-10: TARO-2 88.27+-1.6% robust / 93.41+-1.1% clean; TARO-3 88.57+-0.9% robust / 93.45+-0.9% clean; AAOpt 85.12+-0.7% robust / 91.53+-0.9% clean; undefended 0.0% robust
PGD-EOT-20 l2 (eps=0.5) CIFAR-10 WRN-28-10: TARO-2 69.21+-0.7% robust; TARO-2-AA 88.19+-1.2%; TARO-2+Consistency 88.99+-1.2% (best); AAOpt 86.40+-0.4%
BPDA-EOT l_inf CIFAR-10 WRN-28-10: TARO-3-AA 93.04+-0.8% robust / 93.59+-0.7% clean (best); AAOpt 91.36+-0.8% / 92.10+-0.7%
BPDA-EOT l_inf CIFAR-100 WRN-28-10: TARO-3-AA 72.41+-1.7% robust; AAOpt 66.20+-1.8%
DiffBreak low-frequency attack CIFAR-10 WRN-28-10: 1-step Denoise 0.0%; DiffPure-EDM 0.0%; AAOpt 85.41+-4.4%; TARO-2-AA 90.10+-1.5% (best)
Full-reverse AutoAttack-EOT CIFAR-10 WRN-28-10: TARO-2-AA 87.50% robust / 91.01% clean (best); AAOpt 85.54% / 89.06%; Score-Opt-O 74.22% / 89.84%

Guidance-strength ablation (Table 1, PGD-EOT-20 CIFAR-10 WRN-28-10): l_inf robust accuracy peaks at gamma=1.5 (87.82% with consistency) and falls to 58.13% at gamma=3.0 (with consistency); l2 robust accuracy with consistency rises from 75.51% (gamma=0.5) to 89.05% (gamma=1.5) to 86.39% (gamma=2.0)
Stop-gradient ablation (Table 7): TARO-3 87.13% robust without stop-gradient vs 88.57% with stop-gradient, showing gains are not from gradient obstruction

CLIPure corrected-evaluation case study (Table 9, AutoAttack l_inf eps=8/255): CLIPure-Cos reported 91.1% robust drops to 0.0% under graph-consistent (create_graph=True) attack; CLIPure-Diff reported 88.0% drops to 0.0%
baselines_compared: Adversarial training: Pang et al. 2022, Gowal et al. 2020, Gowal et al. 2021, Wang et al. 2023, Rebuffi et al. 2021, Augustin et al. 2020
Standard adversarial purification: DiffPure/Nie et al. 2022, Yoon et al. 2021, Lin et al. 2025, Li et al. 2025 (ADBM), Hill et al. 2021, Wang et al. 2022
Test-time optimization purification: LM/Chen et al. 2024, Score-Opt (Zhang et al. 2023, both -O and -N variants), AAOpt (Wesego and Rooshenas 2025, reimplemented under matched settings)
CLIPure (Zhang et al. 2025) as an evaluation-methodology case study
